disease.name: Colorectal Bowel Obstruction
disease.note: Mechanical obstruction of the large bowel. The three most common causes in adults are colorectal cancer (60%), diverticulitis (15%), and volvulus (10%). In the Kenyan setting, late presentation of colorectal cancer is common, and many patients present with established obstruction. Always check for a competent ileocecal valve — if competent, a closed-loop obstruction develops with risk of cecal perforation.
pathophysiologyDetail: Mechanical obstruction creates a closed-loop when the ileocecal valve is competent — the cecum bears the highest wall tension (Law of Laplace: tension = pressure × radius) and is the most common site of perforation
Cecal diameter >12 cm correlates with impending perforation — transmural pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure (32 mmHg), leading to ischemic necrosis of the antimesenteric border

Accumulation of gas (from swallowed air and bacterial fermentation, predominantly nitrogen and hydrogen) accounts for 70% of dilated colonic volume; fluid from impaired mucosal absorption accumulates in the lumen
Bacterial overgrowth with E. coli, Bacteroides, and anaerobic species leads to production of hydrogen and methane gas, exacerbating distension and contributing to mural inflammation
Mucosal ischemia triggers neutrophil activation with release of myeloperoxidase, reactive oxygen species, and matrix metalloproteinases that degrade the extracellular matrix
Loss of mucosal barrier integrity permits bacterial translocation through the lamina propria into the portal and systemic circulations — the first step toward sepsis
Competent ileocecal valve creates a true closed-loop with progressive increase in intraluminal pressure, first compromising venous outflow (congestion and edema), then arteriolar inflow (ischemia)

Incompetent ileocecal valve allows decompression into the small bowel — the small bowel dilates but the cecal pressure remains lower, reducing perforation risk but causing more severe vomiting and dehydration

Colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's) results from autonomic imbalance — excessive sympathetic (splanchnic) input and reduced parasympathetic (sacral) output, leading to loss of coordinated colonic motility without a mechanical transition point
Prostaglandin E2 and nitric oxide overproduction in the inflamed colonic wall cause smooth muscle relaxation and loss of tone, contributing to dilation beyond the site of obstruction
Volvulus creates a closed-loop with vascular pedicle twisting — venous occlusion occurs first (within 30 minutes), followed by arterial occlusion (within 6-12 hours) leading to transmural necrosis
Malignant strictures from colorectal cancer often present with incomplete obstruction that progresses to complete — the tumor's desmoplastic reaction causes gradual luminal narrowing over months
Diverticulitis-related obstruction involves a combination of inflammatory phlegmon, edema, and stricture formation — the obstruction is often partial and may resolve with medical therapy
Ischemic colitis can cause obstruction-like symptoms due to severe mural edema and thumbprinting, though the underlying mechanism is vascular insufficiency rather than mechanical blockage
Chronic radiation proctopathy/colitis can induce progressive endarteritis obliterans, leading to ischemic stricture formation and delayed obstruction months to years after pelvic radiotherapy

landmarkTrials.trial: COST Trial — Lap vs Open Colectomy for Colon Cancer
landmarkTrials.population: 872 patients with colon cancer across 48 institutions
landmarkTrials.finding: No difference in 5-year overall survival (76.4% vs 74.6%) or recurrence (20% vs 19%) between laparoscopic and open colectomy
landmarkTrials.impact: Established laparoscopic colectomy as a safe oncologic alternative to open surgery
landmarkTrials.reference: Clinical Outcomes of Surgical Therapy Study Group. N Engl J Med 2004;350:2050-9

landmarkTrials.trial: CLASICC Trial — Lap vs Open for Colorectal Cancer
landmarkTrials.population: 794 patients with colorectal cancer randomized to laparoscopic vs open surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: Laparoscopic conversion rate 29%. Equivalent oncologic outcomes at 3 years. Higher rate of positive CRM in laparoscopic group for rectal cancer (12% vs 6%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed safety of laparoscopic colectomy but raised caution about laparoscopic rectal cancer surgery

landmarkTrials.trial: ESMO Guideline — SEMS as Bridge to Surgery
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 10 RCTs comparing emergency surgery vs SEMS for malignant left-sided obstruction
landmarkTrials.finding: SEMS bridge-to-surgery increased primary anastomosis rate (OR 2.5), reduced stoma rate (OR 0.3), but no difference in 5-year survival
landmarkTrials.impact: SEMS is recommended as bridge to surgery for left-sided malignant obstruction in eligible patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Arezzo A, et al. Surg Endosc 2018;32:2980-93

landmarkTrials.trial: Neostigmine for Acute Colonic Pseudo-Obstruction — RCT
landmarkTrials.population: 21 hospitalized patients with Ogilvie's syndrome, cecal diameter >10cm, no mechanical obstruction
landmarkTrials.finding: Neostigmine 2mg IV produced prompt decompression in 89% vs 0% placebo (p<0.001). Recurrence rate 11% at 72h
landmarkTrials.impact: Neostigmine became first-line pharmacologic therapy for acute colonic pseudo-obstruction
landmarkTrials.reference: Ponec RJ, et al. N Engl J Med 1999;341:137-41

landmarkTrials.trial: Hartmann vs Primary Anastomosis for Emergency Left Colon Surgery
landmarkTrials.population: 300 patients with left colon emergency surgery randomized to Hartmann vs resection + primary anastomosis + covering ileostomy
landmarkTrials.finding: Primary anastomosis had comparable mortality (7% vs 9%) but significantly lower stoma rate at 12 months (23% vs 72%). Higher leak rate 5% vs 2%
landmarkTrials.impact: Primary anastomosis with covering ileostomy is feasible in selected emergency left colon resections
landmarkTrials.reference: Oberkofler CE, et al. Ann Surg 2015;262:891-8

landmarkTrials.population: 340 patients with obstructing left colon cancer randomized to SEMS bridge vs emergency surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: SEMS group had higher successful primary anastomosis (92% vs 67%), lower overall complications (32% vs 54%), similar oncologic outcomes
landmarkTrials.impact: Strengthened evidence for SEMS bridge-to-surgery approach in obstructed left colon cancer
landmarkTrials.reference: Kim JS, et al. Colorectal Dis 2016;18:173-80

landmarkTrials.population: 311 patients with Hinchey III diverticulitis randomized to laparoscopic lavage vs sigmoid resection
landmarkTrials.finding: Laparoscopic lavage had inferior outcomes: higher reoperation rate (19% vs 5%), higher missed perforation rate. No mortality difference
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic lavage should NOT be used for generalized purulent peritonitis from diverticulitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Schulz JK, et al. Br J Surg 2020;107:121-30

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT abdomen with IV contrast is the gold standard imaging for large bowel obstruction with >95% sensitivity
Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) as bridge to surgery for left-sided malignant obstruction is safe and effective
evidenceTable.reference: Arezzo A, et al. Cochrane Review 2017

evidenceTable.recommendation: Emergency resection is indicated for obstruction with perforation, ischemia, or generalized peritonitis
evidenceTable.reference: WSES Guidelines for Obstructed Colon Cancer, 2020

evidenceTable.recommendation: Cecal diameter >12cm on abdominal X-ray indicates impending perforation and requires urgent intervention
evidenceTable.reference: Surgical teaching consensus; Lowman RM, Davis L, Surg Gynecol Obstet 1956

evidenceTable.recommendation: Neostigmine 2mg IV is first-line pharmacotherapy for acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's) after failed conservative measures
evidenceTable.reference: Ponec RJ, et al. N Engl J Med 1999

evidenceTable.recommendation: Hartmann procedure is preferred for obstructed left colon with fecal peritonitis or severe contamination
evidenceTable.reference: Oberkofler CE, et al. Ann Surg 2015

evidenceTable.recommendation: Primary anastomosis with covering ileostomy is an alternative to Hartmann in selected hemodynamically stable patients
evidenceTable.reference: Oberkofler CE, et al. Ann Surg 2015

evidenceTable.recommendation: Lactate >2 mmol/L in large bowel obstruction raises concern for ischemia and warrants urgent surgical intervention
evidenceTable.reference: Level II evidence; multiple cohort studies

evidenceTable.recommendation: Endoscopic decompression (colonoscopy) is indicated for Ogilvie's syndrome failing neostigmine therapy
Colonoscopy should follow resolution of diverticulitis-related obstruction at 6-8 weeks to exclude malignancy
Water-soluble contrast enema can differentiate mechanical obstruction from pseudo-obstruction when CT is equivocal
evidenceTable.reference: Chapman AH, et al. Clin Radiol 2007

evidenceTable.recommendation: SEMS is contraindicated in perforated obstruction, multiple sites, or distal rectal tumors involving the anal canal
evidenceTable.reference: ESGE Guidelines for Colonic Stenting, Endoscopy 2016

evidenceTable.recommendation: Incompetent ileocecal valve on CT indicates lower risk of cecal perforation compared to competent valve

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Patient presents with abdominal distension, constipation, colicky pain
→ Take history: last passage of stool/flatus, prior surgeries, weight loss, rectal bleeding, previous episodes, diet, occupation
→ Physical examination: distension, tenderness, peritonism, hernial orifices, DRE
→ Perform abdominal X-ray (supine + erect)
  │
  ├── Dilated colon (haustra visible) → LARGE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION
  │     → Measure cecal diameter (>12cm = high risk)
  │     → Assess for coffee bean sign (volvulus)
  │     → No small bowel air = competent IC valve (closed loop)
  │     → Small bowel also air = incompetent IC valve (lower risk)
  │
  ├── Dilated small bowel with valvulae conniventes → SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION
  ├── No dilation → PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION?
  │
→ CT Abdomen with IV contrast
  │
  ├── Transition point found → MECHANICAL OBSTRUCTION
  │     ├── Mass/cancer → STAGING: CEA, CT chest, MDT
  │     │     ├── Left-sided, no peritonitis → SEMS bridge to surgery
  │     │     ├── Right-sided → Resection + primary anastomosis
  │     │     └── Peritonitis/sepsis → Emergency Hartmann or primary anastomosis+stoma
  │     │
  │     ├── Volvulus signs → WATER-SOLUBLE CONTRAST ENEMA
  │     │     ├── Bird beak → Sigmoid volvulus → Endoscopic detorsion → Elective resection
  │     │     └── Cecal volvulus → Emergency laparotomy
  │     │
  │     ├── Diverticulitis signs → Hinchey Classify
  │     │     ├── I-II → IV antibiotics, percutaneous drainage if abscess >4cm
  │     │     └── III-IV → Emergency surgery (Hartmann or resection + stoma)
  │     │
  │     └── Ischemic colitis signs → CT angiography → Medical or surgical
  │
  └── No transition point → PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (Ogilvie's)
        → Correct K+, Mg2+, Ca2+
        → NBM, NG tube, rectal tube
        → Neostigmine 2mg IV (cardiac monitoring)
        → If fails → Colonoscopic decompression
        → If fails → Surgical (cecostomy or subtotal colectomy)

managementPearls.pearl: Do not operate on a patient with large bowel obstruction without adequate resuscitation. These patients are profoundly dehydrated from vomiting and third-space losses. Resuscitate with 2-3L crystalloid, correct electrolytes, and insert an NG tube before going to theatre.

Cecal diameter >12 cm on supine abdominal X-ray is a surgical emergency. Do not wait for peritonitis. The Law of Laplace dictates that the cecum (largest radius) will perforate first. Once the mucosa is ischemic, perforation is imminent.
managementPearls.evidence: Lowman RM, Surg Gynecol Obstet 1956; accepted surgical dogma

managementPearls.pearl: For left-sided malignant obstruction being managed with SEMS bridge-to-surgery, perform definitive resection within 5-14 days after stenting. Longer delays risk stent migration, tumor ingrowth, and perforation. Shorter intervals don't allow adequate decompression.
managementPearls.evidence: ESGE Guidelines 2016; Arezzo A, et al. Cochrane Review 2017

managementPearls.pearl: Key features of pseudo-obstruction: hospitalized patient, recent surgery or medical illness, absence of colicky pain, no transition point on CT. A water-soluble contrast enema will show patency to the cecum. Do NOT perform colonoscopy for decompression without first ruling out mechanical obstruction — the risk of perforation is catastrophic.

A previous history of similar self-resolving episodes is highly suggestive of recurrent volvulus. The coffee bean sign on X-ray is pathognomonic. In a resource-limited Kenyan setting where CT may not be immediately available, a water-soluble contrast enema can both diagnose and treat sigmoid volvulus.

Neostigmine 2mg IV given over 3-5 minutes with cardiac monitoring produces prompt decompression in 80-90% of pseudo-obstruction patients. Atropine 0.6mg should be available for bradycardia. Have the patient supine with a bedpan ready — the response can be explosive.
managementPearls.evidence: Ponec RJ, et al. N Engl J Med 1999

managementPearls.pearl: In Kenya, volvulus is more common than in Western populations due to high-fiber diets (ugali, githeri) causing megacolon. Colorectal cancer presents late with obstruction. Always assess for sigmoid volvulus first as it can be managed endoscopically, avoiding emergency laparotomy.
managementPearls.evidence: Local epidemiology studies; Oluoch D, et al. East Afr Med J 2010

managementPearls.pearl: Anastomotic leak after emergency colorectal resection occurs in 5-15%. Risk factors: poor nutritional status (albumin <3.0), steroid use, intraoperative hypotension, and tension. If any two factors are present, strongly consider a covering stoma or Hartmann procedure.
managementPearls.evidence: PROSPER Trial 2020; Oberkofler CE, et al. Ann Surg 2015

managementPearls.pearl: In emergency surgery for bowel obstruction, the bowel is massively distended. Mark potential stoma sites BEFORE making the midline incision. A stoma placed in a skin crease or near the iliac crest will become impossible to manage once the distension resolves.
managementPearls.evidence: Wound Ostomy Continence Nurses Society Guidelines; expert consensus

managementPearls.pearl: For the unstable patient with perforation and peritonitis, do not perform a prolonged definitive operation. Damage control: control contamination (resect perforated segment, stapled ends), pack the abdomen, temporary closure (Bogota bag or VAC), and return to ICU. Definitive surgery in 24-48h.
managementPearls.evidence: WSES Guidelines for Damage Control Surgery, 2019

managementPearls.pearl: When performing a water-soluble contrast enema for suspected obstruction, use GASTROGRAFIN (not barium). Barium extravasation into the peritoneal cavity causes a severe, often fatal, chemical peritonitis. Gastrografin is water-soluble and resorbable.
managementPearls.evidence: ASCRS Guidelines 2021; standard radiology safety protocol

managementPearls.pearl: A simple flatus tube inserted per rectum to 20-25cm and left on free drainage can decompress the colon in Ogilvie's syndrome. This should be the first step before Neostigmine. It takes 10 seconds and has no side effects.
managementPearls.evidence: Level III evidence; standard surgical practice

operativeNuances.pitfall: Rectal stump too long (leaving >5cm of rectum) increases risk of stump blowout and pelvic sepsis
operativeNuances.expertTip: Dissect the rectum to the level of the peritoneal reflection (sacral promontory) and divide there. A 2-3cm rectal stump above the pelvic floor is ideal. Suture the stump to the presacral fascia to prevent retraction. If there is concern, place a transrectal drain into the stump.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Grossly dilated and edematous obstructed right colon makes safe laparoscopic dissection extremely hazardous
operativeNuances.expertTip: For obstructed right colon, strongly consider open approach. The dilated colon is fragile, the mesentery is edematous, and the risk of enterotomy is high. If starting laparoscopic, have a low threshold to convert. Use a wound protector for extraction.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Attempting to traverse a tight malignant stricture with the colonoscope can cause perforation before the stent is deployed
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a through-the-scope (TTS) technique: pass a guidewire through the stricture under fluoroscopic guidance FIRST. Then advance the stent delivery system over the wire. Do not force the colonoscope through a tight stricture. If the stricture is very tight, use balloon dilation to 10-12mm before stent deployment.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Emergency entry into a massively distended abdomen can result in bowel injury — the distended colon is directly under the anterior abdominal wall
operativeNuances.expertTip: Make a small (5cm) incision in the upper midline, enter the peritoneal cavity carefully, then extend the incision downward under direct vision. Have a large-bore needle ready for immediate decompression of the distended cecum if needed (needle cecostomy) to gain working room.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Standard on-table lavage for obstructed left colon is cumbersome, time-consuming, and increases contamination risk
operativeNuances.expertTip: A simpler alternative: resect the obstructed segment, exteriorize the proximal colon, place a purse-string suture, insert a large-bore rectal tube into the open end, and lavage with warm saline through the tube into a bucket. Then perform primary anastomosis. The entire process takes 10 minutes.

operativeNuances.pitfall: A stapled colorectal anastomosis that appears intact may still have a small defect at the corner of the staple line
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always perform an air leak test: fill the pelvis with saline, occlude the proximal colon, insufflate air through a proctoscope or the anal canal. Look for bubbles. If bubbles are seen, reinforce with interrupted sutures. This simple test reduces clinical leak rate from 8% to 2%.

operativeNuances.pitfall: A loop ileostomy created too distally in the terminal ileum may have high output (>1.5L/day), causing dehydration and electrolyte disturbances
operativeNuances.expertTip: Site the loop ileostomy 15-20cm from the ileocecal valve. This preserves enough terminal ileum for absorption of bile acids and vitamin B12. Mark the proximal limb (afferent) clearly — use a suture or marking pen — to prevent later reversal confusion.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Endoscopic detorsion of a sigmoid volvulus without confirming mural viability can miss already-gangrenous bowel
operativeNuances.expertTip: After detorsion, the mucosa should appear pink with visible vascular pattern. If there are patchy hemorrhagic areas, the bowel is compromised. Do NOT simply place a rectal tube and plan elective surgery — the patient needs immediate laparotomy and resection.

prognosis.overallMortality: Emergency surgery for large bowel obstruction carries 5-15% 30-day mortality. With perforation and feculent peritonitis, mortality rises to 20-40%. Pseudo-obstruction with timely management: <5% mortality.
prognosis.morbidity: Overall complication rate 30-50%. Anastomotic leak 5-15%, wound infection 20-30%, wound dehiscence 5-10%, stoma complications 15-25%, prolonged ileus 10-20%, VTE 3-5%.
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrent obstruction: 5-10% at 5 years (adhesions, recurrent malignancy). Stoma reversal after Hartmann: 50-70% reversal rate. Recurrent sigmoid volvulus after resection: <5%.
prognosis.survivalData: For malignant obstruction: median survival for Stage III 60-72 months with adjuvant therapy, Stage IV 18-24 months. For benign causes: normal life expectancy after successful treatment.
prognosis.landmarkReference: WSES Guidelines for Large Bowel Obstruction, World J Emerg Surg 2020; ASCRS Guidelines for Large Bowel Obstruction, Dis Colon Rectum 2021

presentingComplaints: Absolute constipation — unable to pass stool or flatus
Vomiting that is late and may be feculent (brown, foul-smelling)
Previous history of gradually worsening constipation

history_questions.question: When did you last pass stool or flatus?
Did the distension come on suddenly or gradually over days to weeks?
Is the abdominal pain cramping or constant?
Have you had a change in your normal bowel habit before this episode?
Have you noticed any blood in your stool recently?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past few months?
Have you ever been diagnosed with colorectal cancer, diverticulitis, or volvulus?
Do you have a family history of colon cancer?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
What colour is your vomit — clear, green, or brown?
Do you have any fever with chills?
Have you had any previous episodes of partial obstruction that resolved?
What is your appetite like? Are you eating less?
Do you smoke or consume alcohol?
What is your diet like — do you eat meat daily?
Have you ever had a colonoscopy before?
Do you have any chronic illnesses like diabetes or hypertension?
Have you taken any laxatives or traditional remedies for this problem?
Is the pain better or worse after vomiting?
Do you feel bloated all the time or just when the pain comes?
Have you ever been screened for colorectal cancer?
Do you eat ugali, githeri, or other high-fiber maize-based foods daily?
How long did you wait at home before coming to hospital? What did you try first?
Have you visited a traditional healer for this problem?
Do you have any hernia in your groin or belly button?
Are you able to drink fluids or keep anything down?
How many times have you vomited in the last 24 hours?
Is your abdomen getting bigger hour by hour?
Do you feel short of breath when lying flat?
Have you had any recent change in your urine output — is it less than usual?
Do you have pain in your lower back or between your shoulder blades?
Have you ever had abdominal tuberculosis or TB treatment?
For women: have you had any gynaecological cancers or pelvic radiation?
What medications do you take regularly — any blood thinners (aspirin, warfarin)?
Do you take any traditional herbal preparations for your bowels?
Have you ever had a stroke or paralysis that affects your bowel function?
Do you have a known diagnosis of Parkinson's disease or dementia?
Have you traveled recently or had a change in your drinking water?

examination_findings.finding: Massive abdominal distension with visible loops
Tympanic percussion note across the distended colon
High-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds with rushes (early) or absent (late)
Palpable abdominal mass suggesting tumor or fecal loading
Guarding or rigidity indicating peritonitis
Rectal exam — may reveal mass, blood, or empty ballooned rectum
Tachycardia and hypotension if dehydrated or septic
Fever suggesting perforation or abscess
Rebound tenderness indicating peritoneal irritation
Signs of dehydration — dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor
Palpable loops of colon with exaggerated haustra
Succussion splash in obstructed bowel
Hernial orifices must be examined as hernia is a common cause
Peripheral edema and cachexia in malignant obstruction
Oliguria from dehydration
Borborygmi audible without stethoscope in early obstruction
Abdominal splinting with respiration — limited diaphragmatic excursion from distension
Visible peristalsis in thin patients with chronic obstruction
Shifting dullness if concurrent ascites from malignancy
Palpable Virchow's node (supraclavicular) in malignant obstruction
Sister Mary Joseph nodule (periumbilical) in advanced intra-abdominal malignancy
Caput medusae suggesting portal hypertension from liver metastases
Frothing at the mouth or aspiration of feculent material
Cullen's sign (periumbilical ecchymosis) in retroperitoneal hemorrhage
Grey-Turner's sign (flank ecchymosis) in retroperitoneal hemorrhage/pancreatitis
Absent bowel sounds with tympanic abdomen in late obstruction

bedside_scores.name: Large Bowel Obstruction Severity Score

components.name: Competent ileocecal valve (no small bowel air)

bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-3: can attempt conservative decompression; 4-7: urgent intervention needed; 8-12: emergency surgery required

riskFactors.note: Adhesions are more common in small bowel

subtypes.name: Malignant Large Bowel Obstruction
subtypes.management: Staging CT. Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) as bridge to surgery or palliation. Elective resection with primary anastomosis after decompression. Emergency surgery if perforation, failed stenting, or peritonitis.

subtypes.name: Diverticulitis Related Obstruction
subtypes.management: IV antibiotics. CT-guided drainage if abscess. Conservative trial for 48-72h. Surgical resection if no improvement or complication (Hartmann or primary anastomosis).

subtypes.name: Acute Colonic Pseudo-Obstruction (Ogilvie's)
subtypes.management: Conservative: NBM, NG tube, rectal tube, correct electrolytes (K+, Mg2+). Neostigmine 2mg IV (with cardiac monitoring) if no improvement. Colonoscopic decompression if Neostigmine fails. Surgical decompression (cecostomy) if all else fails.

subtypes.name: Volvulus-Related Obstruction
subtypes.management: Sigmoid volvulus: endoscopic detorsion + rectal tube + elective sigmoid resection. Cecal volvulus: emergency laparotomy (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). Transverse colon volvulus: emergency resection.

bedside.name: Nasogastric tube insertion
bedside.indication: decompression, assess output volume and character

bedside.name: Foley catheter
bedside.indication: monitor urine output in dehydrated patient

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray supine + erect
imaging.indication: dilated colon, haustra visible, cecal diameter measurement

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: identify cause, site, degree of obstruction, and assess ischemia

imaging.name: Water-soluble contrast enema
imaging.indication: if CT inconclusive, differentiate mechanical from pseudo-obstruction

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray
imaging.indication: rule out free air under diaphragm (erect film)

interpretation_rules.cecal_diameter: Cecal diameter >12cm on supine abdominal X-ray indicates impending perforation. Law of Laplace: cecum has the thinnest wall and will perforate first.
interpretation_rules.haustral_pattern: Haustra are incomplete bands (vs valvulae conniventes which are complete). In large bowel obstruction, haustra are visible in the distended colon and pericolonic fat stranding may be seen on CT.
interpretation_rules.ct_finding_transition_point: A transition point with collapsed distal colon indicates mechanical obstruction. Absence of transition suggests pseudo-obstruction.
interpretation_rules.sems_decision: Self-expanding metallic stents are appropriate as bridge to surgery for left-sided malignant obstruction. Contraindicated if perforation, multiple sites, or very distal rectal tumor.
interpretation_rules.ileocecal_valve_competence: Competent valve = closed loop = high risk of perforation. Signs: dilated colon without small bowel air. Incompetent valve = small bowel also dilated = lower perforation risk.

management.protocolReference: ASCRS Guidelines for Large Bowel Obstruction (2021)
management.initial: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (crystalloids). NBM. Nasogastric tube for decompression. Correct electrolyte abnormalities (K+, Mg2+). Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Catheterize to monitor urine output. Pain management. CT imaging for diagnosis and staging.
management.definitive: Malignant obstruction: Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) for left-sided lesions as bridge to surgery. Elective resection with primary anastomosis after decompression (7-10 days). Emergency resection (Hartmann or primary anastomosis + covering stoma) if peritonitis/failed stenting. Right-sided lesions: primary resection and anastomosis. Diverticulitis: Hartmann procedure or primary resection with anastomosis. Pseudo-obstruction: Neostigmine, colonoscopic decompression, surgical cecostomy.
management.postop: ICU for haemodynamically unstable patients. Continue IV antibiotics 24-48h. VTE prophylaxis (LMWH). Early mobilization. Monitor NG output and bowel function return. Watch for anastomotic leak (tachycardia, fever, peritonitis, drain contents). Stoma care teaching if stoma created. Dietary advancement as tolerated.

pathways.criteria: no peritonitis, no ischemia, no perforation, cecal diameter <12cm
pathways.treatment: Resuscitation. SEMS for left-sided malignant obstruction. Elective single-stage resection after decompression. For pseudo-obstruction: conservative + Neostigmine.

pathways.criteria: peritonitis, ischemia, perforation, cecal dilation >12cm, systemic toxicity
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Resection with end colostomy (Hartmann) or primary anastomosis with covering stoma. IV antibiotics. ICU admission. Damage control surgery if required.

operative_templates.indication: Obstructing left colon lesion with perforation, peritonitis, or poor bowel preparation
operative_templates.approach: Open midline laparotomy (or laparoscopic if favourable)
operative_templates.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Midline laparotomy — full abdominal exploration
Assess cause and site of obstruction
Mobilize left colon — incise white line of Toldt
Identify and protect left ureter
Divide the affected colonic segment with proximal viable margin
Divide the rectosigmoid at the sacral promontory
Oversew or staple the rectal stump and leave it in the pelvis
Bring the proximal colon out as an end colostomy (left iliac fossa)
Copious peritoneal lavage
Place pelvic drain if contamination present
Apply colostomy bag

operative_templates.procedure: Right Hemicolectomy (for obstructing right colon cancer)
operative_templates.indication: Obstructing cecal, ascending colon, or hepatic flexure tumor
steps: Midline laparotomy, assess for metastases
Mobilize right colon — white line of Toldt
Identify right ureter and duodenum — protect
Divide ileocolic, right colic, and right branch of middle colic vessels
Transect ileum 10-15cm from ileocecal valve
Transect transverse colon
Remove specimen, check margins
Ileocolic anastomosis (side-to-side or end-to-side)
Close mesenteric defect
Place drain, close abdomen

operative_templates.indication: Left-sided malignant large bowel obstruction as bridge to surgery or palliation
steps: Patient in left lateral position
Colonoscopy to the distal tumor margin
Pass guidewire across the stricture under fluoroscopic guidance
Advance the stent delivery system over the wire
Deploy the stent under direct vision and fluoroscopy
Confirm position and expansion with fluoroscopy and endoscopy
Check for complications — free air (perforation), bleeding

dischargeCriteria: No fever or signs of sepsis
Patient and caregiver educated on warning signs and stoma care
Follow-up with surgical oncology team confirmed

patient_education.explanation: Bowel obstruction means that your large intestine is blocked, preventing stool and gas from passing through. This can be caused by a tumour, inflammation, a twisted bowel, or other conditions. In Kenya, many patients present late because they first try home remedies or over-the-counter medications. Delaying treatment can cause the bowel to burst, which is life-threatening. Treatment involves relieving the blockage, sometimes with a temporary stent, and then surgery to fix the underlying cause.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you have: severe worsening abdominal pain, increasing abdominal swelling, inability to pass gas or stool for more than 24 hours after treatment, vomiting (especially brown fluid), fever with chills, or any wound redness/discharge if you had surgery. For stoma patients: no output from stoma for >8 hours, dark or bloody stoma output, or severe cramping.
patient_education.activity: After surgery, start walking as soon as you are able — this helps prevent blood clots and speeds recovery. Avoid heavy lifting (more than 5kg) for 6-8 weeks. Do not drive for 2 weeks after surgery. When you return home, gradually increase activity as tolerated.
patient_education.diet: Start with clear fluids (water, clear soup, tea) and advance to soft foods as tolerated. Eat small frequent meals. Avoid gassy foods (beans, cabbage, soda) initially. After recovery, eat a balanced diet with moderate fiber. If you have a stoma, you will receive specific dietary advice — chew food well and avoid foods that block the stoma (nuts, popcorn, maize kernels).
patient_education.medication: Take all antibiotics and pain medications as prescribed. You may need stool softeners if constipation develops. If you have a stent, there are no specific restrictions. If you have a stoma, clean the bag and skin daily with mild soap and water. Do not take traditional herbal remedies without consulting your doctor.
patient_education.followUp: You will need to see the surgeon in 1-2 weeks for wound check and histology results discussion. If a stent was placed, surgical resection will be planned in 1-2 weeks. You may also see an oncologist if cancer is found. Colonoscopy after recovery is important to check the rest of the colon.

followup.before_discharge: Wound inspection. Confirm oral intake tolerance. Ensure stoma functioning (if applicable). Histology review discussion. Patient and family education on warning signs. Arrange home care (stoma supplies, dressings).

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check, suture removal if non-absorbable. Stoma assessment. Review histology and stage disease. Refer to oncology if indicated.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Plan elective resection if stent placed. Ensure normal bowel function. Nutritional assessment. Stoma therapy follow-up.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery evaluation. Discuss adjuvant therapy if malignancy. Stoma reversal planning if applicable. CT scan for surveillance if cancer.
after_discharge.3_months: Colonoscopy to visualize remaining colon. CEA levels if colorectal cancer. Incisional hernia surveillance. Long-term follow-up plan with oncology.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe abdominal pain not controlled, vomiting, fever, inability to pass stool/flatus for >24h, wound discharge or separation, heavy bleeding, stoma stops working or turns dark, or signs of dehydration (dry mouth, dizziness, dark urine).
